assetTransformations.method: Map source-bounded maritime event states into a unitless non-georeferenced display frame while keeping target, cargo, casualty, zone, and legal claims attributed or unresolved.

claimRevisions.text: The public vessel-strike campaign began with a U.S.-announced attack on September 2, 2025 and later operated under the name Operation Southern Spear.
U.S. officials asserted that the September 2 vessel departed Venezuela, was operated by Tren de Aragua, carried narcotics, and that eleven people were killed.
Later official briefings and reporting state that nine people were killed in the first September 2 strike and two survivors clinging to wreckage were killed in a follow-on strike.

The administration reported Article II and self-defense grounds and later characterized the United States as being in a non-international armed conflict with designated cartels or drug-trafficking organizations.
Operation Southern Spear was formally announced in November 2025, after the first vessel strikes had already occurred.
SOUTHCOM told the Lead Inspector General that it could not provide a publicly releasable accounting of all strikes and interdictions under Operation Southern Spear.
The Lead Inspector General reported that public reporting indicated at least 47 boats were struck between September 2025 and March 2026, with 156 people killed or presumed dead through March 31.
The Lead Inspector General reported that approximately two-thirds of the strikes through March 31 occurred in the eastern Pacific and one-third in the Caribbean.
A record-by-record minimum reconciliation yields at least 66 vessels through June 21 by adding 19 publicly announced April-June vessels to the Lead IG's 47-boat cutoff.
Associated Press reported more than 60 attacks and more than 210 people killed by June 22, 2026.

No single unqualified campaign death total is supportable because public sources use different cutoffs and distinguish immediate deaths, later missing persons, survivors, and presumed deaths differently.
The documented campaign spans the Caribbean Sea and eastern Pacific Ocean; describing all vessel strikes as bombings in Venezuela is geographically overbroad.
U.S. releases often characterize incidents as occurring in international waters, but most public releases do not supply exact coordinates, datum, or a legally precise maritime-zone determination.

Without exact coordinates and applicable baselines, the public record cannot distinguish territorial sea, exclusive economic zone outside territorial sea, and high seas for the reconstructed incident cell.
SOUTHCOM stated that its June 21 Caribbean strike killed two men, left six male survivors, and triggered notification to the Coast Guard search-and-rescue system.

The June 21 release asserts the vessel was operated by a designated terrorist organization and engaged in narcotics trafficking, while the public materials reviewed do not disclose the underlying intelligence or cargo evidence.
The June 21 DVIDS item is an edited government publication, not a preserved native sensor record with public coordinates, camera calibration, platform telemetry, or munition metadata.
U.S. officials reported that an October 14, 2025 strike in waters off Venezuela killed six people and asserted the vessel was trafficking narcotics in international waters.
The Burnley complaint alleges that two people killed in the October 14 strike were Trinidadian fishermen and farmworkers returning home from Venezuela and were not drug traffickers.
Burnley v. United States remained pending without a merits ruling at the July 17, 2026 research cutoff.
The IACHR called for the United States to protect the right to life, due process, and accountability and to investigate deaths and detentions arising from the extraterritorial operations.
UN Special Procedures mandate holders expressed concern that the August authorization and September 2 strike could involve serious violations of the right to life and other international obligations.
The Department of War Inspector General announced an evaluation of SOUTHCOM targeting-cycle procedures but expressly did not frame it as a review of the strikes' legality.
No public complete strike ledger, target packets, incident coordinates, native sensor files, completed campaign-legality investigation, or court merits judgment was identified through July 17, 2026.

claims.proceduralStatus: Campaign boundary for this package; land strikes, tanker seizures, interdictions, and the separate Venezuela operation are excluded.
Attributed U.S. target, cargo, affiliation, origin, and casualty assertions; no underlying public target packet is included.
unresolvedQuestions: What independently verifiable evidence identifies the vessel, occupants, cargo, affiliation, route, and exact position?

claims.proceduralStatus: The existence of a follow-on strike is acknowledged; the survivors' legal status, orders, necessity, and legality remain disputed and unadjudicated.
unresolvedQuestions: What does the complete, unedited sensor record show between the two strikes?
What orders, threat assessment, and survivor procedures governed the follow-on strike?

claims.proceduralStatus: Attributed legal position, not a judicial holding or independent legality finding.
unresolvedQuestions: What is the complete public legal analysis and its incident-specific application?

claims.proceduralStatus: Naming and chronology, not an assessment of authority or legality.
Controlling completeness limit for every aggregate and incident register in this package.
unresolvedQuestions: Will SOUTHCOM publish a canonical incident and vessel ledger with correction history?

claims.proceduralStatus: Official oversight report quoting a public-reporting aggregate, not a complete SOUTHCOM strike ledger.
unresolvedQuestions: Which specific vessels and casualty-status changes compose the 47 and 156 totals?

claims.proceduralStatus: Basin-level aggregate only; exact coordinates are not supplied.
Derived lower bound, not an official campaign total; strike, engagement, and vessel counts can diverge.
unresolvedQuestions: Does a later authoritative ledger confirm the 66-vessel lower bound and its counting unit?

claims.proceduralStatus: Publisher aggregate with its own cutoff and counting rules; it is not silently merged with the Lead IG aggregate.
unresolvedQuestions: How does the publisher count multiple vessels, follow-on strikes, missing persons, and presumed deaths?

claims.proceduralStatus: Editorial counting rule: preserve each source's number, cutoff, unit, and casualty categories.
unresolvedQuestions: Which survivors were later rescued, detained, repatriated, or presumed dead?

claims.proceduralStatus: Some early incidents were described as near, off, or involving routes from Venezuela, but the campaign is not confined to Venezuelan territory.
International-waters wording remains an attributed location claim, not a geospatial solution.
unresolvedQuestions: What coordinates, datum, coastal baselines, and boundary data apply to each event?

claims.proceduralStatus: Legal-geospatial analysis boundary; no circular Venezuela-zone graphic is treated as a finding.
unresolvedQuestions: Can authoritative coordinates and baseline-vintage records be lawfully obtained?

claims.proceduralStatus: Attributed official casualty, target, and SAR account; the release did not report a final rescue outcome.
unresolvedQuestions: Were the six survivors located, rescued, detained, transferred, or later presumed dead?

claims.proceduralStatus: Target and cargo assertions remain attributed; absence of public evidence is not proof that no classified evidence exists.
unresolvedQuestions: What reviewable evidence supports the vessel, occupant, cargo, route, and organization classifications?

claims.proceduralStatus: Publication-provenance fact and missing-data boundary; the video bytes are excluded from this fixture.
unresolvedQuestions: Do native, authenticated files and a complete derivative history exist for qualified review?

claims.proceduralStatus: Attributed official account of the incident later challenged in civil litigation.
unresolvedQuestions: What exact location, vessel identity, cargo record, target packet, and occupant identities support the account?

claims.proceduralStatus: Plaintiffs' allegations in pending civil litigation; no merits finding has resolved them.
unresolvedQuestions: What evidence will be admitted, contested, or withheld if the case reaches merits discovery?

claims.proceduralStatus: Procedural status only; allegations and defenses are not findings.
unresolvedQuestions: How will estate proceedings and sovereign-immunity arguments affect the case?

claims.proceduralStatus: Institutional human-rights assessment and recommendation, not a court judgment.
Institutional communication requesting information; not an adjudication.
Ongoing procedural oversight; no completed legality finding was identified.
unresolvedQuestions: What findings, recommendations, and incident sample will the completed evaluation publish?

claims.proceduralStatus: Search-negative first-pass boundary, subject to correction as records emerge.
unresolvedQuestions: Which records can be released without compromising lawful security interests or personal dignity?

alternateExplanations: Different strike, engagement, and vessel counting units
contradictions.description: The government says it cannot provide a complete public accounting, while oversight and reporting publish partial aggregates and a source-by-source lower bound.
contradictions.title: Complete ledger versus public lower bounds

contradictions.description: Lead IG and AP totals use different dates and casualty categories; neither should overwrite the other.
contradictions.title: Campaign casualty totals

alternateExplanations: Origin, route, and strike location conflated
contradictions.description: Early incidents were framed near or from Venezuela, but the documented campaign extends across the Caribbean and eastern Pacific and usually lacks exact coordinates.
contradictions.title: Venezuela shorthand versus basin-wide campaign

contradictions.description: Official international-waters wording is not enough to choose territorial sea, EEZ outside territorial sea, or high seas without coordinates and baselines.
contradictions.title: International waters versus legal maritime zone

alternateExplanations: Reliable classified evidence not publicly releasable
Correct vessel but disputed occupant or cargo characterization
contradictions.description: SOUTHCOM asserts DTO operation and trafficking; the public release does not expose the underlying intelligence or cargo proof.
contradictions.title: June 21 target and cargo status

alternateExplanations: Government classification supported by non-public evidence
contradictions.description: The U.S. trafficking characterization conflicts with plaintiffs' allegation that two decedents were fishermen and farmworkers returning home; no merits ruling resolves the conflict.
contradictions.title: October 14 occupant characterization

contradictions.description: The acknowledged follow-on strike and survivor deaths raise unresolved factual and legal questions that the public edited record and current oversight do not decide.
contradictions.title: September 2 follow-on strike

findings: Twenty exact source records separate official claims, oversight, institutional legal positions, reporting, and litigation.
The Lead IG completeness warning governs every aggregate.
limitations: No remote body, social-media payload, source video, classified target packet, or paid docket record is retained.
The chronology is representative, not a fabricated complete strike ledger.

findings: All third-party materials are metadata and link only.
The scene contains project-authored abstract geometry and no copied source pixels, impact replay, bodies, faces, or identifying imagery.
limitations: Government-work status does not establish authenticity, completeness, evidentiary reliability, or public-release approval.

findings: The scene is unitless, qualitative, non-georeferenced, and state-based.
No platform, munition, route, target-recognition, impact, identity, cargo, or photogrammetric solve is claimed.
limitations: The scene is unitless, non-georeferenced, non-metric, and not north-aligned; it is not a map, digital twin, or photogrammetric reconstruction.
No native sensor file, complete derivative history, exact clock, coordinates, datum, camera calibration, platform telemetry, or vessel control dimensions were available.
No attack trajectory, launch point, weapon, guidance, platform, sensor, route, speed, or target-recognition inference is shown.
Deaths and survivors are represented only as abstract source-reported states; no impact, body, face, injury, remains, or graphic imagery is depicted.
Territorial sea, EEZ outside territorial sea, and high seas are mutually exclusive hypotheses, not concentric distance claims or findings.
Cargo, vessel status, flag, organization membership, identities, casualty status changes, and final SAR outcome remain source-specific or unknown.

findings: Target, cargo, DTO, international-waters, casualty, and legal-authority claims remain attributed to their sources.
Litigation allegations and institutional legal positions are not presented as merits findings.
Deaths, missing people, survivors, presumed deaths, and SAR outcomes are not silently merged.
limitations: Later releases, casualty-status changes, oversight findings, or court orders may supersede this cutoff.

findings: No crawler, outreach, authentication bypass, private-person research, media ingestion, deployment, or publication was performed.
Operationally precise coordinates, platforms, munitions, surveillance geometry, routes, and SAR locations are excluded.
limitations: A production private workspace still requires authentication, authorization, encryption, retention, audit, and egress controls.

events.description: The U.S. announces a Caribbean strike tied to a vessel alleged to have departed Venezuela; target, cargo, affiliation, casualty, and follow-on-strike details remain source-specific.
events.title: First publicly announced vessel strike

events.description: U.S. officials report six killed; the Burnley plaintiffs later contest the trafficking characterization for two alleged occupants.
events.title: October 14 strike later challenged

events.description: The military campaign receives its formal operation name after the first vessel strikes.
events.title: Operation Southern Spear formally named

events.description: SOUTHCOM reports two killed, one survivor, and SAR notification.
events.title: January 23 survivor incident

events.description: SOUTHCOM reports three survivors and SAR notification after an eastern Pacific strike.
events.title: March 19 survivor incident

events.description: The oversight report later records a public lower bound of 47 boats and 156 killed or presumed dead through March 31 while stating no complete public accounting was available.
events.title: Lead IG aggregate cutoff

events.description: The Inspector General opens a targeting-procedure evaluation that does not purport to decide legality.
events.title: Targeting-cycle evaluation announced

events.description: SOUTHCOM reports two killed, six survivors, and SAR notification; exact position, evidence packet, and survivor result are not disclosed in the release.
events.title: June 21 Caribbean strike

events.description: AP reports more than 60 attacks and more than 210 killed, and corrects the incident basin/date framing in its June 22 report.
events.title: AP current aggregate and correction

events.description: No later SOUTHCOM vessel-strike release, complete public ledger, completed legality review, or civil merits judgment was identified through the cutoff.
events.title: Research cutoff

glossary.definition: Supported within the package by the strongest available source state; not a universal or judicial finding.
A proposition presented as a named source's claim rather than adopted as fact.
The source link and descriptive record are retained without the remote response body.
A relational visual model that is not metric, geodetic, ballistic, or photogrammetric.

warnings: Internal consistency does not authenticate remote source content.
Human and tool actor identities are not cryptographically authenticated.

investigations.purpose: Build a provenance-first, non-graphic first-pass reconstruction of the U.S. lethal vessel-strike campaign that began near Venezuela and expanded across the Caribbean and eastern Pacific, while preserving target and cargo attribution, incomplete public accounting, casualty-category changes, survivor and SAR uncertainty, legal disputes, civil allegations, rights limits, and the absence of metric reconstruction inputs.
scope: Vessel strikes from September 2, 2025 through June 21, 2026
Official assertions, independent reporting, litigation allegations, and institutional legal positions as separate layers
Contradictions, source corrections, dignity, rights, security, and missing-evidence register
Excludes interdictions, tanker seizures, land strikes, and the separate Venezuela operation
investigations.title: Operation Southern Spear vessel strikes - public-record reconstruction

methodology.exclusionRule: No crawler execution, authenticated access, raw third-party body retention, publication, or unsupported identity, guilt, trajectory, or metric inference.
methodology.inclusionRule: Public records and specifically reviewed metadata-only fixtures relevant to the defined investigation scope.
scope: Vessel strikes from September 2, 2025 through June 21, 2026
Official assertions, independent reporting, litigation allegations, and institutional legal positions as separate layers
Contradictions, source corrections, dignity, rights, security, and missing-evidence register
Excludes interdictions, tanker seizures, land strikes, and the separate Venezuela operation

observations.description: The Lead IG public aggregate places roughly two-thirds of strikes through March in the eastern Pacific and one-third in the Caribbean.

locators.value: Strikes and Interdictions, page 10; legal basis, oversight, survivor, and campaign-limit sections

observations.description: The June 21 SOUTHCOM release reports two deaths, six survivors, and notification of the Coast Guard SAR system.

locators.value: Caribbean vessel, claimed target status, two reported deaths, six survivors, and SAR notification

observations.description: The DVIDS publication exposes an edited public video record but not native sensor telemetry, coordinates, camera calibration, platform, or munition metadata.

locators.value: Edited government publication metadata and short video; remote bytes excluded

observations.description: AP reports that the June 21 public statement did not provide evidence that the vessel carried drugs.

locators.value: Current aggregate, June 21 incident, evidence limits, survivor uncertainty, and correction note

observations.description: The Burnley complaint contests the U.S. characterization of two people allegedly killed in the October 14 strike.

locators.value: Case 1:26-cv-10364, Document 1, allegations concerning two October 14 decedents

observations.description: The public SOUTHCOM kinetic-strike archive reviewed through July 17 shows June 21 as the latest vessel-strike release located for this first pass.

locators.value: Public vessel-strike releases through June 21, 2026

packageRevisions.changeSummary: Upgraded to the v2 provenance, confidence, integrity, reporting, and spatial-artifact contract.
